event: Scenes from the Lives of the Virgin Mary and Mary Magdalene
symbols: multiple registers of scenes — the unfolding of salvation history told through connected episodes
Gothic arched framing — the sacred space of the chapel as a portal to heavenly realities
architectural interiors within the scenes — domestic and temple settings grounding sacred events in human life
angelic figures — divine messengers mediating between heaven and earth throughout salvation history
groupings of haloed figures — the communion of saints witnessing and participating in God's plan
description: A chapel wall fresco arranged in two registers beneath a pointed Gothic arch. The upper register contains three scenes separated by painted architectural frames: at left, a gathering of haloed figures in a columned interior; in the center, a scene with figures in an architectural setting with one prominent figure in a doorway; at right, a group of figures in black and white religious habits before a building. The lower register shows additional narrative scenes including what appears to be a reclining figure visited by angels at the right, and other episodes with groups of figures.
theological_description: The Cappella Rinuccini frescoes by Giovanni da Milano present a comprehensive visual catechesis through interwoven narrative cycles from the lives of the Virgin Mary and Mary Magdalene. This pairing is theologically significant: Mary represents the path of sinless grace and obedient faith, while Magdalene represents the path of conversion and penitent love — together they encompass the full range of the Christian journey toward holiness. The multi-register format echoes the medieval understanding of Scripture's multiple senses: the historical events depicted carry moral, allegorical, and anagogical meanings simultaneously. The scenes from Mary's life — likely including her birth, presentation, and the Annunciation — trace the preparation of the vessel chosen for the Incarnation. The Magdalene scenes — possibly including her conversion, communion with the risen Christ (Noli me tangere), and her legendary later life as a hermit — show the transformative power of grace upon a repentant soul. Together the cycles proclaim that sanctity is accessible through both preserved innocence and radical conversion, and that all paths lead to the same Christ.

scripture_refs.reason: The Annunciation to Mary, likely depicted among the Marian scenes in the upper register
The Visitation of Mary to Elizabeth, a common scene in Marian fresco cycles
Noli me tangere — Christ appearing to Magdalene after the Resurrection, likely depicted in the lower register
The anointing by the sinful woman, traditionally linked to Magdalene's conversion and likely part of her narrative cycle
The Magnificat — Mary's canticle of praise expressing the theology of divine reversal that connects both women's stories
Mary of Bethany choosing the better part, traditionally conflated with Magdalene in the Western hagiographic tradition
